meta.title: W420 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine — OM608 diesel, M282 petrol. Real UK costs, van ownership realities, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W420 engine typically costs £1,800–£3,800 supply only, depending on engine — the M282 petrol sits at £1,800–£3,200, while the OM608 diesel commands £2,200–£3,800. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M282 1.3‑litre turbo petrol is the most reliable in the range — it's a modern unit jointly developed by Renault‑Nissan‑Mitsubishi and Mercedes‑Benz, with a good track record and timing chain stretch as its only real watchpoint. The OM608 diesel is durable but injector and DPF issues are common on higher‑mileage vans.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector failure and DPF clogging on the OM608 1.5‑litre diesel, and timing chain stretch on the M282 1.3‑litre petrol. The electric eCitan has its own considerations (battery health, charging infrastructure) but no combustion engine issues.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a commercial van, yes — but maintenance is key. The OM608 diesel is proven but requires attention to injectors and DPF regeneration, particularly on urban‑use vans. The M282 petrol is simpler and has fewer complications. Regular servicing is non‑negotiable on both.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 75hp 108 CDI diesel is underpowered for the W420's size — avoid unless you're using it exclusively for light urban deliveries. Also be cautious with high‑mileage OM608 diesels without documented injector and DPF history.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rough idle or poor fuel economy (OM608 injector failure), DPF warning light (OM608 DPF clogging), cold‑start rattle (M282 timing chain), or major mechanical knock are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the van's commercial value. A £2,200–£3,800 OM608 recon against a £10,000–£20,000 W420 is 11–38% — safe. Against a £6,000 high‑mileage example, the same recon reaches 37–63% — still under the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold, but borderline on higher‑mileage vans. Commercial use value may justify the spend.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM608 diesel can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing, though injectors and DPF are recurring maintenance items. The M282 petrol can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles with regular oil changes and timely chain attention. Electric battery health is the limiting factor on eCitan.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W420 Citan and the Renault Kangoo?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both share the CMF‑CD platform and are built at the same Renault Maubeuge plant. The W420 Citan uses Mercedes‑Benz badging, different front styling, and unique suspension tuning for a more car‑like ride. Engines are identical (Renault‑sourced), and parts are widely shared across the Alliance.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (108 CDI, 112 CDI, 110 petrol, 113 petrol) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Citan W420 — from the 1.5‑litre OM608 diesel to the 1.3‑litre M282 turbo petrol. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the Kangoo‑based van ownership realities that define this generation. Backed by real UK W420 enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes‑Benz Citan W420 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the Citan W420 specifically — spanning panel van and Tourer/MPV variants with diesel, petrol and electric powertrains.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM608 (K9K): 80% confidence — this is the Renault‑developed 1.5 dCi engine used across the Renault‑Nissan‑Mitsubishi Alliance. It's generally robust but known for injector and turbo issues on higher‑mileage examples. M282: 85% confidence — jointly developed by Renault‑Nissan‑Mitsubishi and Mercedes‑Benz, this 1.3‑litre turbo is well‑proven across the Alliance and Mercedes ranges. All combustion engine ratings are PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Pages for each family. Electric powertrain (eCitan) is not rated for reliability in the traditional sense.

overview.intro: The W420 is the second generation of the Mercedes‑Benz Citan, introduced in August 2021 as the successor to the W415. It is a badge‑engineered variant of the third‑generation Renault Kangoo, sharing the CMF‑CD platform and built alongside the Kangoo and Nissan Townstar at the Renault Maubeuge plant in France. The W420 is available in panel van and Tourer/MPV body styles, with the passenger variant also sold as the T‑Class from 2022. The engine line‑up consists of Renault‑sourced 1.5‑litre diesel and 1.3‑litre petrol four‑cylinders, with an electric eCitan variant introduced in 2023.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Renault‑Nissan CMF‑CD • Predecessor: W415 • Successor: None (production ends 2026) • Sibling models: Renault Kangoo III, Nissan Townstar.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The Citan range (W415/W420 combined) generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], with the OM608 diesel and M282 petrol commanding the strongest specialist interest.

items.quote: The highest‑output 1.5 dCi diesel delivers 116hp and 260Nm of torque — ample for the W420's van and MPV duties. It's the most refined diesel in the range, with a proven track record across the Renault‑Nissan‑Mercedes Alliance. Injector and turbo maintenance are the main watchpoints.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want maximum diesel performance from the W420 range.

items.quote: The 1.3‑litre turbo petrol delivers 131hp and 240Nm — genuinely brisk for a compact van. Jointly developed by Renault‑Nissan‑Mitsubishi and Mercedes‑Benz, it's a thoroughly modern engine with good refinement and proven reliability. Timing chain maintenance is the only real demand.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the diesel's DPF complications.

items.quote: The lowest‑output diesel is underpowered for the W420's size — 75hp struggles with a full load. Injector and turbo issues are documented on higher‑mileage examples, and the DPF can clog on urban‑only vans.
items.whoItsFor: buyers on a tight budget who prioritise fuel economy over performance.

items.quote: The Renault‑sourced 1.5 dCi is generally robust but injector and turbo failures are documented on higher‑mileage examples. A full recon is a significant four‑figure bill, and DPF issues add to the running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who understand diesel maintenance costs and are prepared for potential injector or turbo work.

items.quote: The entry‑level petrol turbo delivers 102hp — more than enough for urban and light commercial use. It avoids the diesel's DPF complications and offers lower running costs than the higher‑output 113. The M282 is a well‑proven unit across the Alliance.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol simplicity without the diesel's complexities.

items.quote: The mid‑range diesel is often overlooked in favour of the 116hp 112 CDI — but 95hp and 260Nm deliver solid real‑world performance for most W420 duties, with slightly lower purchase costs than the top‑spec diesel.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel torque without paying for the highest‑output variant.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — most powerful, same costs
Best Petrol — more power, same engine

economicsRule.title: The W420 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200–£3,800 OM608 recon against a £10,000–£20,000 W420 sits at 11–38% of the car's value — well inside the 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Against a £8,000 high‑mileage example, the same recon reaches 28–48% — still viable, but a significant proportion of the van's residual value. Value the van and its service history first.

problems.title: OM608 1.5 dCi Injector Failure
problems.rootCause: The 1.5 dCi's injectors can fail, causing rough running, poor fuel economy, and potential engine damage if ignored — this is a well‑documented issue across the Renault‑Nissan‑Mercedes Alliance.
problems.recommendation: A £500–£1,200 injector service is dramatically cheaper than the £2,200–£3,800 replacement that follows if ignored — address it proactively.

cta.label: Compare OM608 engine prices for your W420 →

problems.title: OM608 DPF Clogging
problems.rootCause: The diesel particulate filter (DPF) can clog on vans used primarily for urban driving — short journeys prevent the DPF from completing regeneration cycles.
problems.recommendation: On a W420 used for urban delivery work, a £300–£800 DPF service is non‑negotiable — ignore it and the van won't restart.

cta.label: Compare DPF service prices for your W420 →

problems.title: M282 1.3 Turbo Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The 1.3‑litre turbo's timing chain can stretch over time, particularly on engines with extended service intervals — a rattle on cold start is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £600–£1,200 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £2,000–£3,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M282 engine prices for your W420 →

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W420 Citan (2021–2026) only. Pre‑facelift W415 Citan (2012–2021) is covered on its own Generation Page. The T‑Class passenger variant shares the same engine line‑up.
